mission.title: Install and Configure ExternalDNS
mission.description: ExternalDNS is an operator that dynamically manages DNS records for Kubernetes resources such as Services and Ingresses across various DNS providers. It is particularly useful for environments where services are frequently created and destroyed, ensuring that DNS records are always up-to-date. Key trade-offs include the need for proper IAM permissions and potential latency in DNS propagation.

steps.title: Add the ExternalDNS Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official ExternalDNS Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add external-dns https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami

```

> This is the official Bitnami chart repository for ExternalDNS.

steps.title: Install ExternalDNS with Helm
steps.description:
This command installs the ExternalDNS operator in the 'kube-system' namespace with the specified version.

```bash

helm install external-dns external-dns/external-dns --namespace kube-system --version 0.20.0 --set provider=aws --set aws.zoneType=public

```

> Adjust the provider and zoneType as needed for your DNS provider.
commands: helm install external-dns external-dns/external-dns --namespace kube-system --version 0.20.0 --set provider=aws --set aws.zoneType=public

steps.title: Create an ExternalDNS Custom Resource
steps.description:
This step creates a Custom Resource (CR) that defines how ExternalDNS should manage DNS records.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: externaldns.k8s.io/v1alpha1
kind: ExternalDNS
metadata:
  name: external-dns
spec:
  provider: aws
  zoneType: public
  domainFilters:
    - example.com
EOF

```

> Replace 'example.com' with your actual domain.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: externaldns.k8s.io/v1alpha1
kind: ExternalDNS
metadata:
  name: external-dns
spec:
  provider: aws
  zoneType: public
  domainFilters:
    - example.com
EOF

steps.title: Verify ExternalDNS is running
steps.description:
This command checks the status of the ExternalDNS pods to ensure they are running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l app.kubernetes.io/name=external-dns

```

> You should see the ExternalDNS pods in a Running state.

uninstall.title: Uninstall ExternalDNS
uninstall.description:
This step removes the ExternalDNS installation and may lead to orphaned DNS records in your DNS provider.

```bash

helm uninstall external-dns --namespace kube-system

kubectl delete -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: externaldns.k8s.io/v1alpha1
kind: ExternalDNS
metadata:
  name: external-dns
EOF

```

> This will remove the ExternalDNS operator but may leave DNS records intact.
> This deletes the Custom Resource associated with ExternalDNS.
commands:
kubectl delete -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: externaldns.k8s.io/v1alpha1
kind: ExternalDNS
metadata:
  name: external-dns
EOF

upgrade.title: Upgrade ExternalDNS
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your current configuration and resources. This step will upgrade ExternalDNS to the latest version.

```bash

helm upgrade external-dns external-dns/external-dns --namespace kube-system --version 0.20.0

kubectl rollout status deployment/external-dns -n kube-system

```

> Replace with the desired version if needed.
> Verify that the upgrade was successful.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create record set'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can occur if the IAM permissions for the AWS account are insufficient.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the IAM role used by ExternalDNS has permissions to create and manage DNS records.

troubleshooting.title: No DNS records created for Services
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may happen if the Services do not have the correct annotations for ExternalDNS.

**Fix:**
Check that your Services have the appropriate annotations like 'external-dns.alpha.kubernetes.io/hostname'.

troubleshooting.title: ExternalDNS pod is CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can occur due to misconfiguration in the ExternalDNS deployment.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the ExternalDNS pod using 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kube-system' for more details.

troubleshooting.title: DNS records not updating
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may be due to caching issues with the DNS provider.

**Fix:**
Verify the DNS provider's settings and consider flushing the DNS cache.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Azure DNS and improved handling of multiple zones.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated the use of 'zoneId' in favor of 'zoneType'.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v0.19.0, ensure to update your configuration to reflect the new 'zoneType' parameter.

resolution.summary: You should see the ExternalDNS pods running with the command 'kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l app.kubernetes.io/name=external-dns' returning a status of 'Running'.
codeSnippets: helm install external-dns external-dns/external-dns --namespace kube-system --version 0.20.0 --set provider=aws --set aws.zoneType=public

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: externaldns.k8s.io/v1alpha1
kind: ExternalDNS
metadata:
  name: external-dns
spec:
  provider: aws
  zoneType: public
  domainFilters:
    - example.com
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the necessary tools installed.
